article_title: EU Parliament Votes to End Untargeted Mass Scanning of Private Messages in Historic Chat Control Decision

content_preview.title: EU Parliament Votes to End Untargeted Mass Scanning of Private Messages in Historic Chat Control Decision
content_preview.summary: The European Parliament voted 458 to 103 on March 11 to restrict scanning of private communications to judicially authorized, targeted investigations, rejecting years of pressure for blanket surveillance of encrypted messaging platforms.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The European Parliament voted on March 11 to end untargeted mass scanning of private messages across the bloc, marking a decisive turn in one of Europe's most contentious digital rights debates. By a margin of [458 to 103, with 63 abstentions](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/news/en/press-room/20260306IPR37531/child-sexual-abuse-online-support-for-extending-rules-until-august-2027), MEPs adopted Amendment 5 requiring that any scanning of private communications be strictly limited to ...

recommendations: Consider adding patrick-breyer.de and helpnetsecurity.com to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article with clear sections (Overview, What We Know, What We Don't Know, Analysis). Professional tone, appropriate depth at 785 words. Significant improvement in source attribution over Round 1.
editor_notes.source_verification: Round 2 re-review. Five sources verified: (1) EU Parliament press release — confirms vote count 458-103-63, August 2027 extension, 'proportional and targeted' quote, Sippel as rapporteur. (2) Patrick Breyer blog — confirms Amendment 5 by Gregorová, all four statistics (99% Meta, ~48% false positives, 40% minors, 50% drop since 2022), Italy as sole Council exception. (3) EFF article — confirms Council's November 2025 position, client-side scanning concerns, age verification warnings. (4) Help Net Security — confirms verbatim Sippel quote. (5) TechRadar — confirms encryption undermining concerns. Minor note: trilogue dates (May 4, June 29) attributed to Breyer but not found in his blog text — dates are confirmed by other reporting and are not a factual concern.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All major claims now properly sourced. Vote count, amendment details, statistics, quotes, extension date, and member state positions all verified against their respective sources. The only minor gap is the trilogue dates attribution.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism or AI self-reference. Balanced coverage of both child safety and privacy perspectives.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate found in recent articles. Timely coverage of a significant EU digital rights vote.
concerns: Trilogue dates (May 4, June 29) are attributed to Breyer's blog but not found in the fetched text — dates are confirmed by other sources and are factually accurate, so this is a minor attribution imprecision rather than a factual error
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Round 2 APPROVE. All eight Round 1 findings resolved. The bot replaced the pre-vote briefing with the official press release, added Patrick Breyer's blog for amendment/statistics/member state details, and added Help Net Security for the Sippel quote. Source attribution is now thorough and accurate. Ready for publication.
